name: OpenBindings Binding Invoker
description: Interface for invoking bindings in specific formats. A binding invoker interprets a binding format (e.g., OpenAPI, AsyncAPI, gRPC, MCP) and knows how to invoke operations through it. invokeBinding is a typed bidirectional I/O operation: the caller streams BindingInvokerInputFrame messages in (starting with an `open` frame that carries the BindingInvocationInput, followed by zero or more `input` frames and a terminating `close`), and the service streams BindingInvokerOutputFrame messages back (zero or more `output` frames plus a single terminal `complete` or `error` frame). The same shape covers unary, server-streaming, client-streaming, and bidirectional bindings; cardinality is observed by how the caller drives the frames, not declared in the contract. Authentication and other runtime prerequisites are not carried in the OBI document; instead, when a binding cannot proceed because required context is missing, invokeBinding fails with a CONTEXT_REQUIRED error enumerating what must be satisfied, and the runtime resolves it into context (typically via openbindings.context-store) and retries. This keeps prerequisites out of the static interface and uniform across every binding format.

FormatInfo.description: Information about a supported binding format.

location.description: Location of the binding source (path or URL).

BindingContext.description: Opaque runtime context for binding invocation, typically sourced from a context store. Well-known credential fields: 'bearerToken' (string), 'apiKey' (string), 'basic' (object with 'username' and 'password'), 'accessToken' / 'refreshToken' / 'expiresAt' for OAuth. Well-known transport fields: 'headers' / 'cookies' / 'environment' (objects with string values) and 'metadata' (object with arbitrary values). Implementations and callers MAY add additional fields for session state, consent flags, or any other invoker-managed runtime data; drivers SHOULD ignore fields they don't recognize.

ContextRequirement.description: One prerequisite that must be satisfied before a binding can be invoked, resolved into context. `type` names the requirement family; the remaining fields are type-specific. The well-known `auth.*` family resolves into BindingContext credential fields: `auth.bearer` produces 'bearerToken', `auth.apiKey` produces 'apiKey', `auth.basic` produces 'basic', and `auth.oauth2` produces 'accessToken' (carrying flow fields such as 'authorizeUrl', 'tokenUrl', and 'scopes'). Other families (e.g. 'approval.user', 'config.value', 'account.link') resolve into their own context fields. A runtime dispatches each requirement to a resolver registered for its `type`.

type.description: Requirement type identifier, e.g. 'auth.bearer', 'auth.oauth2', 'auth.basic', 'auth.apiKey', 'approval.user', 'config.value'. Resolvers register against these; an unrecognized type is unsatisfiable by a runtime that has no resolver for it.

description.description: Human-readable description shown to a user or operator during resolution (e.g. an approval prompt message).

durable.description: Whether resolved context for this requirement MAY be cached under the challenge `key` and reused for later invocations (true; e.g. credentials), or MUST be satisfied freshly for every invocation and MUST NOT be cached (false; e.g. a one-shot user approval). Defaults to true. A requirement family typically fixes this value.

ContextRequiredDetails.description: The `details` payload of an InvocationError whose `code` is CONTEXT_REQUIRED, and the return shape of prepareBinding. Enumerates the runtime context the invoker needs before it can proceed. `alternatives` are disjunctive: satisfying ANY one suffices. The `requirements` within a single alternative are conjunctive: ALL must be satisfied. This OR-of-AND shape captures real authentication semantics such as 'OAuth2 OR (apiKey AND clientCert)', which a flat list cannot.

key.description: Stable context-store key the resolved context is stored under and reused from, typically a normalized target origin (e.g. 'https://api.github.com'). Before invoking, the runtime reads existing context for this key; after resolving, it writes newly-resolved durable context back under it, via openbindings.context-store. Two invocations sharing a key share resolved context. The invoker chooses the key and MUST read the context it uses at invocation time from that same key.

alternatives.description: Disjunctive set of ways to satisfy the requirement. The runtime selects one alternative whose every requirement it can resolve.

requirements.description: Conjunctive set: every requirement in this alternative must be resolved for the alternative to be satisfied.

InvocationError.description: Structured error carried by an `error` output frame. Indicates terminal failure of the invocation.

code.description: Machine-readable error code (e.g., ERR_CANCELLED, ERR_VALIDATION_FAILED, ERR_PROTOCOL, CONTEXT_REQUIRED).

details.description: Optional structured details specific to the error code. For code CONTEXT_REQUIRED, `details` is a ContextRequiredDetails object.

BindingInvocationInput.description: Initial payload carried by the `open` input frame: identifies the binding to invoke and provides context.

BindingInvokerInputFrame.description: One frame from the caller into invokeBinding. The caller streams a sequence: exactly one `open` frame first (carrying the BindingInvocationInput), then zero or more `input` frames carrying values to deliver to the underlying binding, then exactly one `close` frame signaling no more input.

oneOf.title: open
oneOf.description: Open the invocation. MUST be the first frame on the input stream.

oneOf.title: input
oneOf.description: Write one input value to the underlying binding. The value's schema is determined by the underlying binding's operation, not by this contract.

value.description: One input value, opaque at this layer.

oneOf.title: close
oneOf.description: Caller signals that no more input frames will be sent. Idempotent.

BindingInvokerOutputFrame.description: One frame from the service back to the caller. The service streams a sequence: zero or more non-terminal frames (`output`, `input_closed`) followed by exactly one terminal frame (`complete` or `error`).

oneOf.title: output
oneOf.description: One output value from the underlying binding. The value's schema is determined by the underlying binding's operation, not by this contract.

value.description: One output value, opaque at this layer.

oneOf.title: input_closed
oneOf.description: Binding has closed the input side from its end (e.g., a unary binding closes input after the first read). Subsequent caller `input` frames are rejected. The invocation continues; outputs may still flow.

oneOf.title: complete
oneOf.description: Terminal: the underlying binding completed normally. MUST be the last frame.

oneOf.title: error
oneOf.description: Terminal: the underlying binding failed. MUST be the last frame. Carries a structured InvocationError.

listFormats.description: List binding formats this invoker supports.

invokeBinding.description:
Invoke a resolved binding via a typed I/O frame stream. The caller writes a sequence of BindingInvokerInputFrame messages (starting with an `open` frame); the service emits a sequence of BindingInvokerOutputFrame messages ending in either a `complete` or `error` frame. The same shape covers all cardinalities of the underlying binding (unary, server-streaming, client-streaming, bidirectional).

## Normative conformance rules

Implementations of this operation MUST conform to the following rules. These rules apply to both code-module and wire-form implementations; they describe the contract, not any particular transport.

1. **First frame.** The first input frame received MUST be an `open` frame. If the first frame is any other kind, the implementation MUST emit a terminal `error` output frame with code `ERR_PROTOCOL` and process no further input.

2. **Single open.** Exactly one `open` frame is permitted per invocation. If a second `open` frame arrives after the first, the implementation MUST emit a terminal `error` output frame with code `ERR_PROTOCOL`.

3. **Input after close.** If a caller sends any input frame after sending a `close` frame, the implementation MUST emit a terminal `error` output frame with code `ERR_INPUT_CLOSED`.

4. **Input after binding-side input close.** Once the implementation has emitted an `input_closed` output frame, any subsequent input frame from the caller MUST be answered with a terminal `error` output frame carrying code `ERR_INPUT_CLOSED`.

5. **Exactly one terminal output frame.** The output stream MUST end with exactly one terminal frame (`complete` or `error`). The implementation MUST NOT emit further output frames after a terminal frame. If both completion and failure conditions arise during processing, the implementation chooses one terminal frame (typically `error`, since errors take precedence over completion) and emits only that one.

6. **Transport closure without a terminal frame.** If the underlying transport closes without a terminal output frame having been emitted, the consumer side MUST synthesize a terminal `error` frame with code `ERR_TRANSPORT_CLOSED` before propagating closure to its caller.

7. **Discriminator semantics.** The `discriminator` keyword in the frame schemas is informative under JSON Schema 2020-12; validators that don't recognize it MUST still validate frames correctly by evaluating `oneOf`. Implementations dispatch by matching the `kind` property's `const` value against the variant.

8. **`additionalProperties: false`.** Frame variants declare `additionalProperties: false`. Implementations MUST reject input frames with unknown properties (using `ERR_PROTOCOL`). Output frames SHOULD likewise reject extra properties on the consumer side as a protocol violation.

9. **Missing context (CONTEXT_REQUIRED).** If the binding cannot be invoked because required runtime context is missing (credentials, approvals, configuration, and the like), the implementation MUST emit a terminal `error` output frame with code `CONTEXT_REQUIRED` whose `details` is a ContextRequiredDetails object, and it MUST do so before emitting any `output` frame and before causing any observable side effect on the target. This pre-execution guarantee is what makes the runtime's resolve-and-retry safe for non-idempotent operations.

10. **Resolve and retry.** On receiving CONTEXT_REQUIRED, a runtime selects one `alternative` whose every `requirement` it can resolve (alternatives are disjunctive; requirements within one alternative are conjunctive), resolves each requirement into context, persists context for requirements whose `durable` is true (the default) under `details.key` via the context store, and MAY retry the invocation with the augmented context. Context for a `durable: false` requirement MUST NOT be persisted or reused and MUST be re-satisfied on each invocation. A runtime SHOULD bound retries; an implementation SHOULD NOT re-challenge for context it was supplied on the current attempt, and SHOULD instead emit a non-CONTEXT_REQUIRED error when supplied context proves insufficient, so the loop terminates.

11. **Requirement types.** `auth.bearer`, `auth.basic`, `auth.apiKey`, and `auth.oauth2` are the first standard requirement family and resolve into the corresponding BindingContext credential fields. Implementations MAY define further families (e.g. `approval.user`, `config.value`). An unrecognized requirement `type` is unsatisfiable by a runtime that lacks a resolver for it; such a requirement simply makes its enclosing alternative unselectable.

prepareBinding.description: Optional preflight: report the context a binding would require before invocation, without invoking it or causing side effects. Returns a ContextRequiredDetails the runtime can resolve proactively (good for UX, e.g. prompting for auth before a user acts), or null when the invoker cannot determine requirements without invoking (some targets only reveal them via a live CONTEXT_REQUIRED challenge). prepareBinding is advisory: the reactive CONTEXT_REQUIRED error from invokeBinding is the authoritative source of requirements. When `context` is supplied on the input, the result reports only the requirements still unsatisfied by that context.
